key_facts.Custody: Self-custody wallet transaction; bridge trust model depends on route

intent.q: Avalanche Bridge supported networks and assets
intent.a: Avalanche bridging is most often used to move assets between Ethereum and Avalanche C-Chain, with Core also routing users to supported bridge paths for other ecosystems when available. The assets people search for most are ETH, WETH, USDC, USDT, BTC variants, and AVAX liquidity on C-Chain. Support changes by bridge route, token contract, and liquidity, so users should verify the exact asset before signing. The Core bridge interface is the official Ava Labs entry point for checking current routes, balances, and wallet connection state.

intent.q: Avalanche Bridge fees and transfer time
intent.a: Avalanche Bridge cost is not a single fixed fee. A transfer can include source-chain gas, destination-chain gas, bridge or routing fees, and sometimes liquidity-provider pricing if a third-party bridge is used. Ethereum-to-Avalanche transfers are usually dominated by Ethereum gas, while activity after arrival on Avalanche C-Chain requires AVAX for gas. Transfer time also depends on the source chain and the route. Users comparing bridges should check the quoted received amount, estimated arrival time, and whether the route uses canonical messaging, liquidity, or a third-party bridge provider.

intent.q: How to bridge ETH or USDC to Avalanche C-Chain
intent.a: To bridge ETH or USDC to Avalanche C-Chain, connect a self-custody wallet, choose the source network, select Avalanche C-Chain as the destination, and review the exact token contract and received amount before signing. Keep a small amount of AVAX on C-Chain or acquire it after arrival so the asset can be moved or swapped. For USDC, contract versions matter: native USDC, bridged USDC, and older token representations may not be interchangeable in every app. A small first transfer is the practical way to confirm the route and receiving wallet.

Avalanche Bridge historically referred to the official bridge experience for moving assets onto Avalanche, while Core is now the broader Ava Labs wallet and app interface that includes bridge, swap, stake, portfolio, and discover tools. For searchers, the practical answer is that Core is the official current surface to start a bridge action. The underlying route can still involve canonical Avalanche infrastructure or a third-party provider depending on the network and asset. Users should judge the quote by destination chain, token contract, fees, estimated time, and trust assumptions.

faq.q: What is the official Avalanche Bridge now?
faq.a: The official user-facing bridge entry point is Core, the Ava Labs app that includes bridge, swap, stake, earn, and portfolio tools. The older bridge.avax.network domain routes users into the Core bridge experience. Users should still review the route shown in the app because supported networks, token contracts, fees, and third-party routing can vary by asset and direction.

faq.q: Do I need AVAX after bridging to Avalanche?
faq.a: Yes. Avalanche C-Chain transactions require AVAX for gas. If you bridge only USDC, ETH, or another token and have no AVAX on C-Chain, you may be unable to swap, transfer, approve, or interact with DeFi apps. Many users keep a small AVAX balance for basic transaction fees before moving larger assets or interacting with Avalanche protocols.

faq.q: Can I bridge from Coinbase or Binance directly to Avalanche?
faq.a: Centralized exchanges may support withdrawals directly to Avalanche C-Chain for selected assets, but that is not the same as using an onchain bridge. Before withdrawing, confirm the exchange network label, destination address format, token version, and whether the receiving app supports that asset on Avalanche. If the exchange does not support Avalanche for the asset, use a self-custody bridge route instead.

faq.q: Why did my Avalanche bridge transaction not arrive?
faq.a: First check the source-chain transaction hash, then confirm the destination chain is Avalanche C-Chain and not another Avalanche network or external chain. Delays can come from source-chain confirmation time, bridge relayers, liquidity routing, wallet indexing, or a destination transaction that still needs to be claimed. If funds are not visible in a wallet, search the destination address on an Avalanche explorer and verify the token contract.

faq.q: Is Avalanche Bridge only for Ethereum?
faq.a: Avalanche bridge demand is still heavily tied to Ethereum and Avalanche C-Chain liquidity, but modern bridge interfaces can expose more routes depending on integrations. The exact network list changes over time, and different routes may use different providers. The safest answer is to treat Core as the official starting point and confirm the supported source chain, destination chain, and token before signing.

faq.q: What is the difference between X-Chain and C-Chain for bridging?
faq.a: Most DeFi and EVM wallet activity on Avalanche happens on C-Chain, which uses Ethereum-style addresses and smart contracts. X-Chain is Avalanche's exchange chain for asset transfers and has different wallet behavior. When people say they are bridging to Avalanche for DeFi, they usually mean Avalanche C-Chain. Sending to the wrong Avalanche chain can make funds difficult to use without an additional transfer path.
